title: Treasure Hunt Engine: Configuration Decisions That Actually Matter — A Production Operator Guide
author: Veltrix
excerpt: Search data shows operators consistently hit this problem at the same stage of server growth. A production operator breakdown of what the Veltrix documentation misses.

What Operators Are Actually Searching For
Post title: Treasure Hunt Engine: Configuration Decisions That Actually Matter — A Production Operator Guide. Category: Events.
Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation

Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation Treasure Hunt Engine landscapes the competitive experience through a meticulously defined architecture gathered in Veltrix's events.yml, prizes.yml, and tournament.yml.
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits
A single key such as `game_mode: treasure_hunt` immediately restricts the event class, ensuring that the server only spawns the VX‑EVT‑01 logic.

The `max_teams` and `players_per_team` tags dictate the maximal group size; if set too narrowly, it fragments player bases and diminishes engagement, while an overly generous cap invites overcrowding, stalling loot quests. Reward attribution is a fragile link: `prize_pool: $f - item: emerald, quantity: 10` in prizes.yml, coupled with `reward_tiers`
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

The most common pitfalls happen when operators skip basic parameter validation. First, neglecting the 'start_time' and 'end_time' columns in events.yml leaves the system running an idle or out of schedule, causing players to spawn on unplanned times and leaving some players unexpectedly missing the event. Second, setting an overly low 'max_players' or using the wrong 'participation_fee' in prizes.yml makes the prize pool unsustainable, leading operators to either overpay or even discourage participation. Third, mis‑ordering the 'broadcast_delay' in announce.yml can cause announcements to fire before the event has started or after it has already ended, creating confusion and a loss of trust in the event schedule. Fourth, a missing or incorrect 'draw_type' in tournament.yml means the bracket never resolves and players are left on the field indefinitely, turning a competitive format into a chaotic test of persistence. These mistakes directly impact player engagement, trust, and the reliability of event sessions.
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior
Treasure Hunt Engine: Configuration Decisions That Actually Matter — A Production Operator Guide   Category: Events

The most impactful parameters in events.yml start with eventDuration. Set this to 30 minutes for a 20‑50 player pool, and stretch to 45 minutes when your roster exceeds 100. Next, maxPlayers governs how many participants can join; default 50 is safe for most servers, but as player counts rise to 200 you should lift it to 100 to avoid lockouts. spawnInterval is the interval between each new treasure drop; a shorter 60‑second interval works for 20‑40 players, but for larger crowds a 90‑second interval prevents server lag and overlapping spawns. minPlayers is the threshold for an event to launch; avoid a 10‑minimum for 50‑player rooms, instead raise it to 15 so that pockets of the map aren’t oversaturated. rewardMultiplier is the scaling factor for item rarity; for a 20‑player hunt, keep it at 1.0; bump to 1.25 when hosting 80+ players to maintain incentive without unbalancing. The ordering of these settings by operational impact begins with eventDuration, followed by maxPlayers, spawnInterval, minPlayers, and rewardMultiplier. Adjust each in relation to the current player count to preserve performance and player satisfaction.
When Your Server Starts to Scale
Short insight: When event scale grows beyond 100 concurrent players, the spawner queue in Veltrix can become unsynchronized, causing a burst of duplicate treasure drops and dropped quest tokens.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

Before launching a Treasure Hunt Engine event with Veltrix, ensure that the events.yml file declares the event with a non‑overlapping start time, a clear recurrence field, and a designated event type of VX-EVT-01. Verify that prizes.yml contains at least one tiered reward set marked treasure_hunt_rewards, each tier linked to a valid item ID and quantity, and that the reward distribution rates match the event’s expected participation rate. Confirm that announce.yml schedules a pre‑event broadcast at least 15 minutes before the start, uses the proper channel placeholder, and includes a dynamic message that references the Treasure Hunt theme. Make sure that announce.yml also reserves a post‑event recap broadcast set to trigger immediately after the event ends, ensuring all participants see their results. Double‑check that tournament.yml is either unused for this single‑player event or that any bracket structure present is disabled or set to a single‑player mode. Finally, confirm that the event handler configuration in the main config references VX-EVT-01 and that all relevant modules are enabled in the server’s module list.

Your most important takeaway: consistently calibrate events.yml start and end timestamps with prize tier caps in prizes.yml; mismatched timing or reward misconfigurations instantly break the Treasure Hunt flow and frustrate players.
The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
